request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"parse::devtools","name":"parse","one_liner":"MBaaS platform for mobile developers providing cloud storage, push notifications, identity, and custom backend code, acquired by Facebook in 2013 and shut down in 2017.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both are developer-focused B2B SaaS products with usage-metered or consumption-based pricing aimed at backend infrastructure. Parse offered a hosted control plane for mobile app backends; the new pitch offers a hosted control plane for ephemeral preview environments. The pattern of CLI-plus-hosted-plane mirrors Parse's developer UX closely. The difference is that the new pitch is purely cloud-infra tooling rather than a full MBaaS, so the surface area and lock-in dynamics differ somewhat."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both target software developers building cloud-hosted products. Parse targeted mobile developers specifically (globally), while the new pitch targets developers managing multi-cloud preview/staging infrastructure — a narrower, platform-engineering-adjacent niche. The developer customer type and global geography match, but the sub-sector (mobile backend vs. cloud DevOps/preview environments) is distinct enough to lower the score."},"gtm":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both companies used a bottom-up, developer-first GTM motion: free/low-friction onboarding via CLI or SDK, viral adoption through individual developers, then expansion to teams and enterprises. Parse grew rapidly through developer communities and word-of-mouth; the new pitch's CLI-first approach is structurally the same. The difference is that the new pitch targets a more platform-engineering buyer in addition to individual devs."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Parse raised a $5.5M Series A in 2011 and reached 20,000 developers within roughly a year, suggesting early-scale traction. The new pitch appears to be a fresh venture with no stated traction. Stage similarity is moderate — both are early-stage developer infrastructure plays — but Parse had significant early momentum and was acquired before scaling challenges became terminal."}},"why_similar":"Both are developer-focused, hosted control-plane infrastructure products with usage-metered monetization, a CLI-driven developer experience, and a global developer customer base. Parse pioneered the pattern of abstracting backend complexity behind a simple API/SDK for developers; the new pitch does the same for ephemeral cloud preview environments. Both rely on bottom-up developer adoption and face the same core challenge: avoiding commoditization by cloud hyperscalers who can bundle equivalent functionality for free.","where_diverged":"1. Sub-sector: Parse was a full MBaaS (mobile backend-as-a-service) covering storage, auth, push, and compute for mobile apps; the new pitch is narrowly scoped to ephemeral preview environments across cloud providers — a CI/CD and platform-engineering workflow tool, not a general backend platform. 2. Shutdown mechanism: Parse was killed by a deliberate acquirer (Facebook) strategic decision, not by market failure or competition in the traditional sense; the new pitch has no acquirer and must survive on standalone revenue. 3. Multi-cloud scope: The new pitch explicitly spans multiple cloud providers, whereas Parse was a single-cloud (its own) hosted service. 4. Timing: Parse operated in 2011–2017 when mobile-first development was nascent; the new pitch enters a mature cloud-native ecosystem with established competitors (e.g., Vercel, Railway, Render).","failure_causes":["acquirer strategic deprioritization","Facebook/Meta internal tooling substitution","no path to standalone profitability post-acquisition","dependency concentration in a single corporate owner","forced migration burden alienated developer community","commoditization risk from cloud hyperscalers"],"lessons_for_input":["Avoid single-acquirer dependency: build for standalone profitability so the product survives without a strategic parent.","Architect for multi-cloud portability from day one so users are never forced into a painful migration if you shut down or are acquired.","Lock in enterprise platform-engineering teams early with SLAs and dedicated support before hyperscalers (AWS, GCP, Azure) bundle equivalent preview environment tooling.","Maintain an open-source core or clear data-export story to reduce the churn spike that killed Parse's community trust when shutdown was announced.","Differentiate on workflow integration depth (CI/CD pipelines, PR automation, secrets management) rather than raw environment spin-up speed, which is easier to commoditize."]}
